title: Survey Design — Platform-Behavior Mapping
description: The same 30-question consumer behavior survey used for the IoB research paper had enough commercial specificity in it to be read separately. The questions captured not just platform usage but product categories, purchase frequencies, and brand interaction types — which made the dataset useful for e-commerce intelligence, not just behavioral research.

dataTable.caption: Survey Design Parameters
rows: Platform, screen time, product category, purchase frequency, spend range

title: 1,438 Responses — Commercial Signals in Raw Data
description: After 14 months, 1,500 responses were collected and 1,438 verified. Initial data cleaning and exploratory analysis revealed immediate commercial signals: November dominated purchases (13.64%), adults aged 20–24 peaked at 9.116% of all orderers, and 78.28% of respondents spent over ₹4,000 per order.

title: IoB Paper Published — Platform Influence Confirmed
description: The academic paper confirmed the core thesis: social media time directly scales purchase influence. >2 hour users were 45.08% buy-influenced, vs 38.53% for <1 hour users. Instagram led all platforms at 75% average engagement. The paper gave the dataset academic credibility — but the commercial intelligence embedded in the raw data was still untapped.

barChart.caption: Purchase Influence by Time on Social Media

title: E-commerce Lens — Segmenting by Platform x Demographic
description: Extended analysis beyond the paper scope. Segmented the dataset by product category, platform, and demographic to surface commercial patterns. Key insight: Instagram is the dominant discovery platform for adults, but conversion behavior differs by age group. Mature users (40+) show 73.72% Instagram engagement but lower impulse purchase rates than Adults.

figures.alt: Social media platform usage by age group
figures.caption: Platform usage segmentation: Instagram leads across all age groups, but the Adult cohort shows the highest LinkedIn overlap (67.24%) — suggesting a dual-channel discovery behavior relevant to B2B and premium e-commerce. Snapchat and Facebook maintain consistent presence across all cohorts (~50–68%).

figures.alt: Time on social media vs purchase influence
figures.caption: Purchase influence gradient by time-on-platform: each additional time bracket increases the buy-influenced rate by ~3–4 percentage points. Optimizing for session depth (not just reach) is the core e-commerce takeaway: users who spend >2 hours on social media are 6.55 percentage points more likely to purchase than <1 hour users.

title: Seasonal & Category Intelligence — Diwali as a Planning Anchor
description: Diwali (November) accounts for 13.64% of annual online orders — nearly 2× the average monthly rate. Home appliances show a strong gender × age interaction: adult males exhibit 31.07% direct purchase intent (platform-uninfluenced), while adult females show 30.20% platform-influenced discovery behavior. These patterns directly inform inventory timing, ad-spend calendars, and creative strategy.

figures.alt: Home appliance purchases by gender, age group, and social media influence
figures.caption: High-value category behavior by demographic: Adult males show the highest uninfluenced direct purchase intent (31.07%) — indicating brand-loyalty driven decisions not dependent on social discovery. Adult females show the strongest platform-influenced behavior (30.20%). Actionable for targeted ad campaigns: serve discovery ads to female cohort, performance ads to male cohort.

dataTable.caption: Seasonality & Spend Intelligence
rows: Double ad-spend budget in Oct–Nov; run Diwali-specific campaigns
Premium product placement is viable; AOV optimization focus
New-grad cohort: highest intent, best LTV growth opportunity
Retargeting after 2+ session exposure maximizes conversion probability

title: Behavioral Framework for E-commerce Strategy
description: The behavioral patterns from 1,438 real consumer responses now form an actionable commercial framework: use platform time as the primary segmentation variable for targeting; plan inventory and budgets around the November Diwali anchor; segment creative by gender (discovery vs. direct intent); and prioritize 20–24 year olds as the highest-ROI acquisition cohort. Research that began as academic behavioral profiling became a commercial intelligence asset.
